siteMetadata.title: Hi, I'm Changi
siteMetadata.description: Studying Vision & Image Across the World
siteMetadata.about:
Welcome to the Changi website! 
    Currently, I work as an engineer at Samsung Electronics, System LSI in South Korea. My research interests are focused on practical image processing that can address various problems worldwide.
    I enjoy sharing ideas with others, particularly through brainstorming sessions. It's truly enjoyable to exchange random thoughts and develop them into innovative ideas.
    Thank you for visiting my website again. Below, you'll find more information about me!

markdownRemark.excerpt: To deploy a deep learning model in real-life, it is necessary to compress the model for real-time operation. There are several techniques for optimizing deep…
markdownRemark.html:
To deploy a deep learning model in real-life, it is necessary to compress the model for real-time operation. There are several techniques for optimizing deep learning parameters, including pruning, knowledge distillation, and quantization.

  1. Pruning: This involves removing certain neurons or connections in a neural network that are found to be less important for its performance. The goal is to reduce the number of parameters in the model, which can make it faster and more computationally efficient. There are several types of pruning, including weight pruning and neuron pruning.
  2. Knowledge distillation: This is a technique for transferring knowledge from a larger, pre-trained model (the “teacher”) to a smaller model (the “student”). The student model is trained to mimic the outputs of the teacher model, with the idea being that the smaller model will have a similar level of accuracy while being more computationally efficient.
  3. Quantization: This involves converting the weights and activations of a neural network from high precision numbers (e.g., floating point numbers) to low precision numbers (e.g., 8-bit integers). This can reduce the memory requirements and computation time of the model, while still preserving its accuracy.

These are the basic concepts of each compression technique. While they are pretty easy to understand, it would be difficult to write it in code. However, I suppose that it is really important in terms of practical usage and we must be able to do this work because machine learning models are getting bigger and bigger nowadays. Thus, I am going to practice it from this post.

I’ve heard that the pruning is quite easier to use than other compression techniques and produces effective results. So, I will design a simple fusion network first, and compress the network using pruning in Pytorch. Eventually, the final goal is to implement the network on FPGA or small AI computer (e.g., Jetson Nano).

However, due to the lack of my knowledge, I need a teacher to success this project. Basically, I don’t know how to implement pruning in Pytorch. So, I asked “ChatGPT”, which is the most famous conversation A.I. model as well as my teacher, how to implement pruning in Pytorch. The below is his answer.

To implement pruning in PyTorch, you'll need to perform the following steps:
    1. Train a model: First, you'll need to train a deep learning model on your dataset as you normally would. This will give you a pre-trained model to work with.
    2. Choose a pruning method: There are several methods for pruning in deep learning, including weight pruning, neuron pruning, and layer pruning. You'll need to choose the method that is most appropriate for your model and task.
    3. Determine the pruning criteria: You'll need to determine the criteria for pruning, such as the magnitude of the weights or the impact of pruning on the accuracy of the model.
    4. Prune the model: After you've determined the pruning criteria, you can prune the model by removing the weights or neurons that meet the criteria. In PyTorch, you can prune the weights by setting the values to zero, or you can remove the neurons by modifying the model architecture.
    5. Fine-tune the model: After pruning, you'll need to fine-tune the model by continuing the training process. This will help the model adjust to the pruned architecture and regain its accuracy.

Here's some sample code that demonstrates how to perform weight pruning in PyTorch:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Create a model
model = nn.Linear(10, 10)

# Train the model
# ...

# Choose a pruning criterion (e.g. the magnitude of the weights)
weights = model.weight.data.abs()
threshold = weights.mean() + 2 * weights.std()

# Prune the weights
model.weight.data[weights < threshold] = 0

# Fine-tune the model
# ...

He is so sweet. The procedures are not much difficult but there is a point that I can’t understand. As you can see the sample code, ChatGPT just set pruned weights as 0. It doesn’t mean “removing certain neurons or connections”, just the weight value is set as 0. So, I think that it is not able to compress network and the network can’t be operated in real-time. Thus, I asked Mr.ChatGPT again and the answer came as below. 

Yes, you're correct. Simply setting the weights to zero is not the same as actually removing them from the model. In practice, this can make the model more difficult to use because you have to keep track of the zeroed-out weights and handle them differently during inference.

To properly implement pruning in real-life, you'll need to modify the model architecture to actually remove the weights and neurons that you've pruned. This can involve updating the forward pass of the model to exclude the pruned weights and neurons, and updating the optimizer to only update the remaining weights.

Here's an example of how you might implement weight pruning in PyTorch, with the added step of actually removing the pruned weights:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

# Create a model
model = nn.Linear(10, 10)

# Train the model
# ...

# Choose a pruning criterion (e.g. the magnitude of the weights)
weights = model.weight.data.abs().clone().detach()
threshold = weights.mean() + 2 * weights.std()

# Get a mask indicating which weights to prune
mask = (weights >= threshold).float()

# Remove the pruned weights
model.weight.data *= mask

# Create a new optimizer that only updates the remaining weights
optimizer = optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01)

# Zero-out the gradients of the pruned weights in the optimizer
for name, param in model.named_parameters():
    if 'weight' in name:
        param.grad *= mask

# Fine-tune the model
# ...
Note that the exact implementation will depend on the details of your model and pruning method, but this should give you an idea of the general process.

I think I roughly understood. However, because ChatGPT is not always correct, I will write code based on his sample codes and execute it in the next post.
